Among the main ratios, UVV’s ROE declined in both FY2024 and FY2025 due to an expansion in the denominator (the book equity, which increased due to higher retained earnings net of buybacks) and a decrease in the numerator caused by a higher effective tax rate of 26.6% in FY2025.

The following two charts provide evidence of what I introduced in the first paragraph regarding the contribution of the company’s two main segments to both sales and operating income:

The Tobacco segment still dominates the income statement in both indicators, but Ingredients is increasing, with the management underlining that it will receive higher investments in the following years.

Moving on to the capital structure of the company, Universal Corporation holds approximately $1.08B of debt at Q3 FY2026, composed of $462M of short-term notes and $617M of long-term loans. On the other hand, the company has $1.48M in shareholders’ equity, with an implied D/E ratio of 42%. Given the seasonal nature of the business and the high working capital financing required by the business, if we net out the short-term notes related to tobacco-financing activities, the D/E ratio of UVV declines to 25%.

It is fundamental to highlight the 99 years (since 1927) of unbroken dividends distributed by the company. Such dividends have also been increased for 19 consecutive years. Currently, the dividend is running at $0.82 per quarter, implying a $3.82 annualised rate. Contrary to FY2024, when UVV bought back 100k shares, no buyback activities were registered in FY2025.

What I Am Expecting From the Next Quarter

The last earnings release for Q3 FY2026 came out on Feb. 9, 2026, and the Q4 release is expected for May 27, 2026.

In Q3, UVV reported 9M revenues of $2.21B (down 2% YoY), operating income of $183M (down 3% YoY), and net income of $75.9M (down 11% YoY). These results were obviously not encouraging, especially if we look at Q3 alone, with operating income down 21% YoY due to declines in the volume of tobacco sold, combined with inventory write-downs affecting dark-air-cured tobacco leaves.

However, comments by the management remained positive, especially on full-year demand, with the company disclosing a 250M refinance in its credit facility, which will provide UVV with more liquidity at longer maturities.

Regarding Q4 FY2026, it is usually the strongest quarter for the company in terms of revenues, so I am expecting sales to come in at $~2.9B for the whole FY2026, basically flat compared to FY2025. Regarding operating income, I am counting on $235-245M, translating to diluted EPS around $4-4.30, up from the FY2025 levels of $3.78, which were impacted by the $14M in the pension settlement charge.

I am interested in any possible disclosure by the management regarding three key points:

  • The dynamics of UVV’s end markets and the status of the tobacco industry in general, since the management highlighted in Q3 the existing probability of an oversupply event, which can be temporary or structural.
  • The status of the company’s inventory after the dark-air-cured write-downs.
  • The strategy regarding the development of the Ingredients segment, which slowed down a bit during the last quarter. Understanding if this decline is caused by a temporary absorption of fixed costs or by a less responsive market will be fundamental to judging possible future trajectories.

Regarding dividends, I am expecting a slight increase, with the company extending its streak of consecutive dividend growth for the 20th year. I, personally, don’t think buybacks will be announced for this year.

Valuation

I decided to use a DCF method, together with a relative valuation (trading multiples) and a DDM, to derive an implied share price for Universal Corporation.

DCF

Following Damodaran’s approach, I built the cost of equity for the company using the unlevered sector beta levered at UVV’s D/E ratio and tax rate (0.85), combined with a blended equity risk premium of 4.23%.

Using the weighted capital structure of the company, the company’s pre-tax cost of debt from the last 10-K of 6.0%, and the FY2025 tax rate of 26.6%, I obtained a WACC of 6.26%, increased by 1% to reflect some risks I consider real for the company, such as the customer concentration and the stock’s illiquidity premium.

For the FCFF estimation, I started from the 9M FY2026’s revenues, on top of which I added the estimated revenues from the next quarter. I then modelled a 2% nominal sales growth per year, obtained by considering a 1% decline in the global cigarette consumption, offset by an increase of 0.5% in pricing power and ~3% expected growth from the Ingredients business. I assumed a recovering operating margin of 8.7% by FY2028 (for the moment, I think both the sector’s oversupply and the company’s write-offs are temporary, but my assumptions can change after the next earnings release). CapEx is assumed at $65-70M per year (comparable to FY2024 and FY2025), with related D&A of $60-66M. Terminal growth is low, assumed at 1% for a declining market.

The table below summarises the assumptions:

Adding cash ($85.2M) and subtracting debt ($1.08B) and minority interests ($42.4M), I obtained an implied share price of $62.46 per share.

The sensitivity table below shows how the share price changes with changes in the WACC and in the TGR:

The key assumptions here are the investors’ opinions regarding the status of the tobacco industry in the future: investors convinced of a declining business in a declining industry can fairly opt for a Hold or even a Sell judgment; the numbers would support their view.

I, personally, think that the industry is not a high-growth one for sure, but I don’t think it is shrinking that much to justify terminal growth rates of less than one. My DCF base case remains at $62.46 per share.

Relative Valuation

In terms of peer groups, Universal Corporation can be compared to both major tobacco companies, such as Altria, Philip Morris International, and British American Tobacco, and B2B players operating more in the agricultural processes, such as The Andersons, Bunge, and Archer-Daniels-Midland.

I built the following comparison table with multiples coming from the April 2026 consensus estimates on Yahoo Finance. I chose P/E, EV/EBITDA, P/B, and dividend yield as the most important metrics for the relative valuation.

Applying interquartile ranges to EPS, book value per share, and EBITDA, I obtained the following share price results:

The company trades below the median Tobacco company in terms of P/E ratio, with the median EV/EBITDA multiple acting as a lower bound for the valuation.

Dividend Discount Model

Given the 99-year-old dividend history of the company, applying a valuation method focused on the equity-return side of the business is appropriate.

Given the current annualised dividend of $3.28 per share and an estimated growth rate of 2.5% in the long period (based on a 2-3% average CAGR in the past 10 years), the Gordon growth model implies a $63.73 implied share price.

The current market price of $53-54 per share is implicitly assuming a cost of equity of ~10%, higher than the bottom-up cost of equity I built above.

Football Field

The chart below summarises the blended valuation for the stock, combining the DCF, trading multiples, and DDM.

The estimated share price is around $65, against the current $53-54 per share. In other words, the stock is currently trading at ~21% discount from the implied fair value.

Limits and Risks

Of course, my valuation and my thesis are exposed to some risks, some of which I have anticipated earlier in the article.

Firstly, the UVV business is highly centred around a few key customers, with more than 50% of its revenues from tobacco concentrated in 5 manufacturers. Obviously, losing even one of these customers would drastically impact the company’s revenues.

According to the Nicotine Resource Consortium (cited on pg. 38 of the company’s 2025 Annual Report), the consumption of cigarettes in the US has been falling 2%, and the company is exposed to a slow but consistent decline in the industry, which will affect the growth in revenues.

If the industry oversupply continues in the future, there will be other write-downs in the company’s inventory, with dark-air-cured tobacco being the most probable victim. As a consequence, the company’s operating income would shrink considerably.

Conclusion

I consider Universal Corporation the classic “boring, defensive, and dividend-heavy” company, but with a huge red flag determined by a slowly declining industry. The growth perspectives are not favourable, and they will largely depend on how well the Ingredients segment will perform, even if nowadays it represents too small a part of the company to be considered attractive for new investments. The dividend is appealing, but not sufficient to justify, alone, a buy rating.

I advise current investors to hold the stock, continuing to benefit from the dividend cash flow, and consider additional investments in the company if the share price declines to around ~$50. Likewise, new investors can enter at a similar target price.

I will update my position after the Q4 earnings release.

data.title: Dell's Shift Is Bigger Than The Rally Suggests
data.description:
Dell Technologies (DELL) continues to be an attractive buy for me despite the strong bull run because the story has evolved so much that the market has yet to catch up with the change. This is no longer a company whose valuation was driven by potential AI growth. With a backlog of $43 billion and AI-related order intake at $64 billion, there is no shortage of growth here, with a large portion of it baked into the equation. Paying ~14-16 times earnings at ~20%+ growth rate means that I am effectively paying market valuations for a company that enjoys extremely good visibility.

Why the Revaluation of Dell Makes Sense

It is clear why there has been a flurry of attention on Dell lately. The financial numbers that the company reported are quite compelling, with 342% y/y growth in AI-optimized servers revenue, $113.5 billion in total revenue, and an EPS of $10.30, all very good indicators. Nevertheless, none of these constitute news. The point is that Dell has made a fundamental change to its business model within less than one year. This justifies the re-rating.

The most crucial part of Dell's shift is the forward-looking change. The company had $64 billion worth of AI orders at the end of FY2026 and ended the fiscal year with a backlog of $43 billion worth of AI orders. That is not just a pipeline of orders. That is already committed demand that will turn into revenue over the course of several upcoming quarters.

That is why I find the distinction between pipeline and backlog crucial. While companies active in the AI space are valued on expectations, Dell is increasingly doing it on execution. Management is guiding towards revenues of $140 billion in FY2027 and $50 billion in AI servers. Those numbers are no longer speculative; they are backed by orders already made by Dell's clients. That cuts out one of the biggest concerns of growth investing, timing.

Why Dell Has Transitioned From Being a Cyclical Company Into Being Structural

What is crucial about Dell Technologies' business model at the moment is that it plays a much more structural role in the ecosystem. It has ceased being a mere participant and become part of the infrastructure. That positioning is far more valuable than any individual product that Dell can deliver.

In essence, Dell is helping companies like Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, and Meta to deploy their AI infrastructure. At the same time, Dell sees a rapid expansion in the number of enterprise users; the company now has over 4,000 AI clients on board.

I believe that the story is much simpler than that. Dell's core advantage is not the technical knowledge and skills to compete and succeed in such an environment. What differentiates Dell from the competitors is the ability to successfully implement the solution for a client, from the procurement of components and financing to actual deployment and maintenance.

The question that poses itself here is margins. The business of manufacturing AI servers is nowhere close to being a lucrative business, with its dependence on commodity parts, capital intensity, and falling prices. It’s actually a very real concern and one of the key reasons that I’ve always had reservations regarding Dell.

There’s some fresh news on the matter that makes things different, though. ISG operating margins reached 14.8% in Q4, and the management guides AI-related margins to remain at the 5% mark. While that does not seem particularly impressive, it becomes relevant if we account for revenue growth and potential attach rate. That is not the key issue for me. What is more important than the level of margins is the evolution of the margin mix.

As the company transitions to providing AI-centric products, it begins to include storage, networking, deployment, and services in the offer. All of them have higher margins than the products that the company sells to enterprise customers, and the margins should grow with revenue and attach rate. In other words, what we see here is an evolution of Dell's offering—moving from selling boxes to selling full-scale solutions.

How Dell Creates Shareholder Value

On one hand, the company returned $7.5 billion to shareholders in Q4 ($5 billion spent on buybacks and $2.5 billion on dividends). On the other hand, Dell is also actively investing in AI infrastructure and improving its supply chain. That is why I see the lack of downside risk as one of the advantages of the company.

While I do believe that Dell still has upside despite the recent re-rating of shares, this is no longer a speculative investment. The market has recognized the quality and visibility of growth. The company's earnings are set to grow significantly, and the revenue has already been guaranteed by commitments from clients.

Even though the company has experienced a sharp re-rating, Dell Technologies is trading close to the market multiples. If you look at a P/E of 13-15 times on FY2027-FY2028's estimated earnings, you will see that Dell is offering market-level multiples on a revenue growth of ~20%+ and a $43 billion AI backlog.

It is important to note how earnings and thus multiple compress with growth as EPS increases from ~$12.9 currently to ~$14.7 in just one year. As a result, the forward multiple will contract quite substantially, creating a built-in cushion against any multiple compression risk. Meanwhile, an EV/Sales of ~1x for a company whose product is becoming essential in AI infrastructures seems to be undervalued.

When the Mix Cracks, the Story Changes

The critical risk that I am looking at is the ability for ISG to maintain its margins. This particular thesis would hold water if Dell could keep its operating margins in the teens while at the same time growing its AI revenue. Anything lower would indicate that the company has been unable to improve its mix, making its AI servers more a function of volume than a platform play.

The memory price increases will put additional pressure on the bottom line. DRAM and NAND continue to be volatile in nature; should input costs outpace repricing, then margin erosion is bound to happen, despite the management's history of being nimble with prices.

Lastly, the composition of the backlog is important. Should all the growth become increasingly concentrated into a few hyperscalers, then that would defeat one of the key aspects of my thesis, namely, diversification.

Bottom Line

Dell Technologies has moved to becoming a structural player in AI infrastructure quicker than I expected, and I feel more optimistic as a result. The recent stock price re-rating is justified; however, upside still lies in execution. As long as Dell keeps delivering on margins and executes backlog successfully, I would be looking to invest.
